audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Exodus / Shemot text tradition, Song of the Sea poetic layer; draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Song of the Sea, Miriam, women with timbrels, Marah, healing statute/testing.

verses.restored: Then Moses and children of Israel sang this song to YHWH, and they said: I will sing to YHWH, for rising, he has risen; horse and rider he hurled into sea.
Yah is my strength and song, and he became for me salvation/healing; this is my Divine, and I will beautify/praise him, Divine of my father, and I will exalt him.
YHWH is man of war; YHWH is his name.
Pharaoh's chariots and his force he threw into sea; his chosen officers sank in Sea of Reeds.
Deeps cover them; they went down into depths like stone.
Your right hand, YHWH, majestic in power; your right hand, YHWH, shatters enemy.
In greatness of your majesty you tear down those rising against you; you send your burning anger; it consumes them like stubble.
By breath/wind of your nostrils waters piled up; floods stood like heap; deeps congealed in heart of sea.
Enemy said, I will pursue, overtake, divide spoil; my soul/life will be filled with them; I will draw my sword; my hand will dispossess them.
You blew with your breath/wind; sea covered them; they sank like lead in mighty waters.
Who is like you among gods, YHWH? Who is like you, majestic in holiness, feared/revered in praises, doing wonder?
You stretched out your right hand; earth swallowed them.
In your loyal love you led the people whom you redeemed; in your strength you guided them to your holy habitation.
Peoples heard; they trembled. Labor-pain seized inhabitants of Philistia.
Then chiefs of Edom were dismayed; mighty ones of Moab, trembling seized them; all inhabitants of Canaan melted.
Terror and dread fell upon them; by greatness of your arm they became still as stone, until your people passed over, YHWH, until people you acquired passed over.
You will bring them and plant them on mountain of your inheritance, place for your dwelling that you made, YHWH, sanctuary, Lord, that your hands established.
YHWH will reign into age and beyond.
For horse of Pharaoh with his chariot and horsemen went into sea, and YHWH returned waters of sea upon them; but children of Israel walked on dry ground in midst of sea.
Miriam the prophetess, sister of Aaron, took the timbrel in her hand, and all the women went out after her with timbrels and dances.
Miriam answered them: Sing to YHWH, for rising, he has risen; horse and rider he hurled into sea.
Moses made Israel journey from Sea of Reeds, and they went out to wilderness of Shur; they went three days in wilderness and found no water.
They came to Marah, but they could not drink waters of Marah, for they were bitter; therefore its name was called Marah.
The people grumbled against Moses, saying, What shall we drink?
He cried to YHWH, and YHWH showed him a tree/wood; he threw it into waters, and waters became sweet. There he set for him statute and judgment, and there he tested him.

He said, If listening, you listen to voice of YHWH your Divine, and do what is straight in his eyes, give ear to his commands and keep all his statutes, then all the diseases I placed upon Egypt I will not place upon you, for I am YHWH your healer.
They came to Elim, and there were twelve springs of water and seventy palms; and they camped there by waters.

translation_notes: Song of the Sea is ancient victory poetry; preserve its intensity while not turning it into ethnic hatred.
Ruach/wind/breath imagery links sea deliverance to Divine power.
Miriam is explicitly prophetess and leads women with timbrels and dances; preserve her leadership.
Marah moves from victory song to wilderness thirst; YHWH as healer closes the chapter.
The tree/wood sweetening bitter waters should be preserved without over-allegorizing in the source layer.
safety_note: The victory song celebrates liberation from Pharaoh's chariot army, not hatred of Egyptians as a people. Violent poetic imagery should be read as ancient liberation hymn against oppressive military pursuit.
